title: Today's Tech Brief: This AI startup says it can tell if a script will make a hit film
summary: AI startup Quilty claims to predict box office success, Blue Origin explosion still making headlines, and Impulse raises money.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for This AI startup says it can tell if a script will make a hit film
imageCaption: This AI startup says it can tell if a script will make a hit film (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I've got some interesting tech news for you today. Let's start with a story that's making waves in the film industry. An AI startup called Quilty claims it can predict whether a script will make a hit film. According to a report on The Verge, Quilty's algorithm can analyze a script and determine its potential for box office success. This could be a game-changer for Hollywood, where the stakes are high and the failure rate is steep. I mean, who wouldn't want to know if their script is going to bomb or not?

In space news, the Blue Origin explosion is still making headlines. As Ars Technica reports, the incident is still under investigation, but it's clear that it was a significant setback for the company. On a more positive note, Impulse Space has raised money to fund its own space ambitions. The details are still emerging, but it's exciting to see new players entering the space industry. It's like a whole new frontier out there – pun intended.

And finally, I've been thinking about these stories and wondering what's next for AI in the creative industries. Can an algorithm really predict whether a film will be a hit? It's not quite that simple, but it's an intriguing idea. And in space, it's clear that there's still a lot of work to be done to make commercial space travel a reality. But hey, at least we're making progress, right?

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
